title: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
concept: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) takes the absolute value of each error and averages them, treating all errors proportionally without over-penalizing outliers.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What happens to the penalty of an error in MSE when the error size doubles?
exercises.explain: Right — since MSE squares errors, doubling the error size (e.g. 2 to 4) quadruples the penalty (4 to 16).

cards.title: Walking blocks
cards.body: If you walk 5 blocks east or 5 blocks west, you still walked exactly 5 blocks. The direction doesn't matter, only the distance.

cards.title: What is Mean Absolute Error?
cards.body: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) calculates the average of the absolute differences between predictions and actual values, treating all errors proportionally.

cards.title: Taxi meters
cards.body: MAE is like a strict taxi meter. Every block you travel costs exactly $2, whether it's your first block or your fiftieth block. The cost increases steadily.

cards.title: Linear penalties
cards.body: If your model is off by 2 units, the MAE penalty is 2. If it is off by 10, the penalty is 10. The penalty is exactly 5 times larger for an error 5 times bigger.

cards.title: Not always superior
cards.body: People think MAE is always better because it is simpler. However, because it doesn't penalize huge errors extra hard, it might allow a few massive, dangerous errors.

cards.title: MSE vs. MAE penalties
cards.body: Comparing the penalties for errors of 1 and 5:

left.text:
Error of 1 -> Penalty of 1
Error of 5 -> Penalty of 25

right.text:
Error of 1 -> Penalty of 1
Error of 5 -> Penalty of 5

exercises.prompt: How does Mean Absolute Error (MAE) handle a prediction error of -6 units?

options.text: It strips the negative sign, resulting in a penalty of 6
It squares the error, resulting in a penalty of 36

exercises.explain: MAE uses absolute values, which convert negative errors into positive distances.

exercises.prompt: True or false: MAE penalizes large errors exponentially more than small errors.
exercises.explain: False! MAE penalizes errors linearly. An error of 10 gets exactly twice the penalty of an error of 5.

exercises.sentence: MAE is less sensitive to ___ because it does not square the errors.
exercises.explain: Because MAE treats error linearly, extreme outlier values don't warp the final cost score as much.

exercises.prompt: For errors of -1, 2, and -3, what is the calculated Mean Absolute Error (MAE)?
exercises.explain: The absolute errors are 1, 2, and 3. Their sum is 6, and the mean is 6 divided by 3, which is exactly 2.00.

exercises.prompt: You are predicting house prices. A few luxury mansions in your dataset sell for $5 million more than your model predicts. How will MAE handle these huge errors compared to MSE?

options.text: MAE will not let these few luxury houses pull the prediction line away from normal houses as much as MSE would
MAE will panic and shift the prediction line completely to fit only the mansions
MAE will fail because absolute values cannot calculate millions

exercises.explain: Excellent! Because MAE penalizes errors linearly rather than quadratically, it is much less sensitive to extreme outliers than MSE. Next: let's compare them directly to choose the right one!
